text: The surgeon uses their left hand to pull the suture while pushing with the back of the needle driver to efficiently advance the suture. The suture is consistently pushed to one side to avoid crossing it. The bedside assistant palpates the skin, revealing the thin subcutaneous tissue layer.

The surgeon lays out the suture across the defect without initially tightening it, utilizing the width of the defect to manage the suture length effectively. Once the entire strand is laid out, the surgeon begins tightening the suture, allowing for additional suture length to be used for further suturing.

The surgeon demonstrates the progressive tensioning concept by laying out sutures along the length of a large defect and gradually ratcheting each one to distribute tension evenly. This technique is applied at the caudal end of the defect.

The surgeon tightens the suture at the edges of the linea alba, which are close together. As the procedure progresses to the wider portion of the defect, the surgeon lays out the suture across the defect without tightening it, allowing for multiple suture bites of the fascia to be ratcheted and bring the linea alba together.

The surgeon reaches the wider portion of the defect and requests a new 24-inch suture. They advance slightly, ensuring the first suture will eventually bring the linea alba together. Initial suture bites avoid the sac for easier approximation, while subsequent bites incorporate small portions of the sac to prevent large seroma formation.

The surgeon uses a twenty-four inch suture to progressively tension the defect, transforming a single large seroma into multiple smaller seromas. The suture is laid out across the defect and quickly used up, demonstrating the efficiency of this technique.

The surgeon observes that the linea alba is becoming difficult to visualize as the rectus muscle medializes and the linea alba retracts relative to the muscle. This occurs due to the vertical orientation of the rectus muscle from the costal margin to the pubis.

The surgeon leaves the defect open and lays out sutures across the widest part of the defect, ensuring the linea alba remains visible. As the sutures are laid out, the surgeon begins tightening the defect, focusing on the upper cephalad portion without introducing additional sutures.

The surgeon leaves the needle in place and begins pulling each suture strand. The right hand pulls perpendicular to the abdominal wall while the left hand braces the tissue, allowing for controlled tension without breaking the suture or damaging the fascia. The strands appear looser now, facilitating the re-approximation of the defect. The extra suture length is used to finish suturing the re-approximated linea alba between the first and second strand, with gentle tension applied throughout.

The surgeon reduces the pneumoperitoneum pressure to facilitate instrument reach and parks the needle before proceeding to the next suture strand. The cranial portion of the defect is addressed, with re-approximation of small holes and weak fascia pockets. The surgeon stops suturing where the widest part of the linea alba tapers to a more normal diastasis.

The surgeon revisits earlier suture strands, adjusting them as they progress from caudal to cranial and back to caudal, demonstrating the closure of larger defects. The needle is visibly parked to keep track of which strand belongs to which needle, preventing confusion during the suturing process.

The surgeon manages multiple strands of suture by walking back the initial two sutures and cutting them to prevent entanglement with the third suture. The sutures are placed on the posterior rectus sheath edge, with some being walked back entirely and others locked with a few throws.

The surgeon examines the patient's abdominal contour, noting the visible diastasis and bulge. The surgeon continues placating the abdominal wall until the bulge is no longer visible. The surgeon ensures a four-centimeter mesh overlap by dissecting and dividing more of the posterior rectus sheath to achieve adequate coverage.

The surgeon uses a systematic approach to defect closure, employing a progressive tensioning method with long strands of V-lock suture. The surgeon pulls perpendicular to the tissue while bracing with the left hand, periodically checking the appearance of the abdominal wall at the bedside to ensure satisfaction with the bulge reduction.
